agent.root_goal: Produce a bilingual (EN/KR) developer style guide for 'Cheonma', an internal payments platform, in two independently-owned parts. Part A (platform standards team): first survey the naming actually used by Cheonma's three legacy services (ledger, checkout, merchant-portal), recording every legacy identifier, endpoint name, and error code in use; then decide and write the canonical terminology standard (endpoint nouns, error-code vocabulary, casing rules). The published standard lists ONLY the new canonical terms, not the legacy ones. Part B (developer relations team): write the worked integration tutorial with runnable code samples, covering four sections in both languages: the checkout flow, the refund flow, error handling, and migration notes for teams upgrading from the legacy services - the migration notes must map each legacy identifier to its canonical replacement, old -> new. Company handbook rule, applying to the whole guide: documentation ships only if every identifier, endpoint name, and error code appearing anywhere in the guide matches the published canonical standard exactly; term drift between parts is a shipping defect.

action.reasoning: The root task requires producing a bilingual developer style guide split into two parts: Part A (canonical terminology standard) and Part B (integration tutorial with migration notes). Part B depends on Part A because the tutorial must use the canonical terms exactly, and the migration notes must map legacy identifiers to the canonical replacements defined in Part A. Therefore, I will spawn two subtasks: root.1 for Part A (survey and standard) and root.2 for Part B (tutorial), with root.2 gated on the completion of root.1. I will take the self_role of integrating the two parts and producing the final assembled guide index to ensure term consistency and fulfill the root-level deliverable.

subtasks.goal: Survey legacy naming across ledger, checkout, and merchant-portal services and produce the canonical terminology standard.
subtasks.capsule: This subtask acts as the platform standards team. It must survey the three legacy services (ledger, checkout, merchant-portal) to record every legacy identifier, endpoint name, and error code. Then it decides and writes the canonical terminology standard covering endpoint nouns, error-code vocabulary, and casing rules. The published standard lists ONLY the new canonical terms. This output is the single source of truth for terminology used in the entire guide.

outputs.description: Comprehensive record of all legacy identifiers, endpoint names, and error codes from ledger, checkout, and merchant-portal services.
The canonical terminology standard listing only new canonical terms for endpoints, error codes, and casing rules. This is the authoritative reference for Part B.

subtasks.goal: Write the bilingual integration tutorial with runnable code samples and migration notes.
subtasks.capsule: This subtask acts as the developer relations team. It writes the worked integration tutorial in both English and Korean, covering four sections: checkout flow, refund flow, error handling, and migration notes. The migration notes must map each legacy identifier to its canonical replacement (old -> new). Every identifier, endpoint name, and error code used must match the canonical standard from root.1 exactly. Term drift is a shipping defect.

outputs.description: English-language integration tutorial with runnable code samples for checkout, refund, error handling, and migration notes.
Korean-language integration tutorial with runnable code samples for checkout, refund, error handling, and migration notes.

self_role.goal: Integrate Part A and Part B outputs into a final assembled bilingual developer style guide index.

outputs.description: Final assembled index linking the canonical standard and bilingual tutorials, confirming term consistency across all parts.

This is a single, coherent intellectual task that does not benefit from decomposition; splitting it would risk inconsistency between the survey and the standard. I will execute the task directly, generating the legacy survey first, then deriving the canonical standard from it.
